# Ergonomics & Biomechanics Data

<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

Supporting data for the French Horn Effort Rubric. All claims cite sources in [citations.md](../citations.md).

---

## Holding Position Comparison

### Bb Clarinet

- **Orientation:** Held vertically in front of body at approximately 35-45° angle from torso
- **Weight bearing:** Primarily on right thumb via thumb rest; left thumb supports from below
- **Arm position:** Both arms forward and slightly down, relatively symmetrical
- **Body twist:** Minimal — near-midline hold
- **Thumb stress:** Significant — the right thumb bears the full instrument weight (~0.7 kg / 1.5 lbs)
  - Neck strap intervention reduces thumb force significantly (p < 0.05) [12 via Smyth & Mirka 2021]
  - Higher thumb-rest position decreases abductor pollicis brevis and flexor carpi ulnaris activity [12 via Young & Winges 2017]
- **Posture effects (Baadjou 2017) [13]:**
  - Experimental (optimized) posture produced smaller thoracic angles and larger pelvic tilt (all p < 0.001)
  - Optimized posture reduced upper trapezius and brachioradialis activity
  - Optimized posture increased erector spinae L3 and lower trapezius activity
  - Sound quality: experts found "no consistent pattern between body posture and sound quality"

### French Horn

- **Orientation:** Held to the right side, bell pointing backward and down (or to the right)
- **Weight bearing:** Left hand operates valves and partially supports weight; right hand inside bell provides counterbalance and support from below; weight distributed across both arms asymmetrically
- **Arm position:** Left arm forward and up to reach valve cluster; right arm extended to the right and back into bell. Significant asymmetric loading.
- **Body twist:** Moderate — the player's torso often rotates slightly right to accommodate bell position
- **Documented strain areas [9][10]:** Neck, shoulders, arms, back
  - Adult players develop postural imbalances including shoulder misalignment and spinal overarch [10]
  - "A lot of players develop problems in their back due to a very heavy horn" [1]
- **EMG data with ergonomic support (Price & Watson 2018) [23]:**
  - French horn showed bilateral reductions principally in **biceps, pectoralis major, and deltoid** when using Ergobrass support
  - Reductions typically 15-30% across many muscles
  - This confirms these are the primary load-bearing muscles during horn playing

---

## Embouchure Force Comparison

From systematic review [12], citing Clemente et al. 2019:

| Instrument | Upper lip force (g) | Lower lip force (g) |
|---|---|---|
| Oboe | 23.0 | 17.0 |
| Bassoon | 6.3 | 10.3 |
| Saxophone | 38.9 | 62.7 |
| **Clarinet** | **58.0** | **54.1** |
| **French horn** | **56.0** | **86.0** |
| Trumpet | 62.7 | 89.2 |
| Trombone | 201.0 | 220.0 |

**Key finding [12]:** "Brass players apply greater force than woodwind players during embouchure." The horn requires 59% more lower-lip force than clarinet (86 g vs. 54.1 g), while upper-lip force is similar (56 g vs. 58 g).

---

## Finger & Hand Forces

### Clarinet Finger Force [12]

From Hofmann & Goebl 2016 (within systematic review [12]):

| Context | Mean Force (N) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Weber concerto excerpts | 1.17 | Higher demand passages |
| Technical exercises (professionals) | 0.54 | Lower force = more efficient |
| Technical exercises (students) | 0.68 | p = 0.213 (not significant) |
| Technical exercises (overall) | 0.64 | — |

### Clarinet Thumb Force

- Neck strap significantly decreases thumb force (p < 0.05), with perceived effort reductions for right shoulder and thumb [12 via Smyth & Mirka 2021]
- No significant changes in neck or shoulder EMG with strap use

### French Horn Valve Force

- No specific valve force measurements found in accessed literature
- Horn uses rotary valves (vs. clarinet's keys), which operate on a different mechanical principle
- String-action rotary valves (as on the H279 [4]) are generally considered lighter than mechanical linkage

---

## Musculoskeletal Disorder Prevalence

### General Prevalence [12]

From the systematic review (Lopez-Pineda et al. 2023):
- **12-month prevalence:** 41-93%
- **Lifetime prevalence:** 62-93%
- Clarinet was the most-studied instrument (appeared in 12 of 16 studies reviewed)

### By Instrument Category [20]

From Cebria i Iranzo et al. 2010 (partially verified — source inaccessible):
- Brass instruments and oboe/bassoon: **low injury rates (0-5.9%)**
- Saxophone, clarinet, flute: **medium injury rates (6.0-11.9%)**

Note: The "up to 86% prevalence" figure originally attributed to this source was NOT confirmed. The paper's own figures are substantially lower (0-11.9% by instrument category). The higher figures likely represent career-lifetime prevalence from other studies examining all musicians collectively.

### Neck and Shoulder Pain [24]

From van Selms et al. 2020 (N=1,470 musicians from 50 Dutch ensembles):
- Neck and shoulder pain overall: **52.5%**
- TMD pain: **18.3%**
- Headache: **42.5%**
- Woodwind players specifically associated with TMD pain

### Children (Ranelli et al. 2011) [26]

N=731 children, ages 7-17:
- Lifetime PRMP symptoms: **67%**
- Past-month symptoms: **56%**
- Right hand/elbow problems: **24%**
- Instrument type significantly associated with all PRMP (p < 0.005)

---

## Masticatory (Jaw) Muscle Activity [18]

From Gotouda et al. 2007. N=19 brass + 14 woodwind players, EMG of masseter and temporal muscles.

**Key findings:**
- Brass players: RMS in high tone significantly higher than tuning tone in all muscles examined
- Woodwind players: RMS in high tone NOT significantly higher than tuning tone
- After 90 minutes of sustained playing: no decrease in mean power frequency in either group — no obvious jaw muscle fatigue
- Masticatory load during playing is "very small" compared to maximum clenching [18]

**Relevance:** Switching from clarinet to horn will require adapting to higher jaw muscle activation at high pitches, but overall masticatory load is small and unlikely to be a limiting fatigue factor.

---

## Posture-Related Muscle Activity

### Clarinet (Baadjou 2017) [13]

N=20 professional and student clarinet players. Cross-sectional study comparing habitual vs. optimized (Mensendieck method) posture.

**Muscles with INCREASED activity in optimized posture:**
- Left and right erector spinae L3 (postural stabilizers)
- Left and right lower trapezius (scapular stabilizers)

**Muscles with DECREASED activity in optimized posture:**
- Left upper trapezius (neck/shoulder elevator)
- Right brachioradialis (forearm)

**Conclusion [13]:** "By increasing stability, a decrease in activity of the upper extremity muscles can be induced."

### French Horn (Price & Watson 2018) [23]

N=20 conservatoire students. EMG with and without Ergobrass ergonomic support.

**Primary load-bearing muscles during horn playing (showing reductions with support):**
- Biceps (bilateral)
- Pectoralis major (bilateral)
- Deltoid (bilateral)

Reductions typically 15-30%. This identifies the specific muscles that fatigue during sustained horn playing. For comparison, trumpet showed primarily left deltoid and trapezius involvement (lighter instrument, less muscle involvement) [23].

---

## Respiratory Posture Effects [12]

From Ackermann et al. 2014 (within systematic review, N=113 wind/brass musicians):
- Higher chest cavity expansion when standing vs. sitting (p < 0.01)
- Lower abdominal cavity expansion when sitting (p < 0.01)
- Lower muscle activation seated vs. standing (p < 0.01)

**Relevance:** Standing produces better respiratory capacity for all wind instruments. This applies equally to clarinet and horn practice.

---

## Awkwardness Scale — Qualitative Assessment

Based on the biomechanical data above, a qualitative-to-quantitative awkwardness comparison:

| Factor | Clarinet | French Horn | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body twist required | Minimal (1/5) | Moderate (3/5) | Horn requires torso rotation |
| Arm extension | Low (2/5) | High (4/5) | Horn requires arms out to sides |
| Support symmetry | Moderate (3/5) | Low (4/5) | Horn is highly asymmetric |
| Thumb/wrist stress | High (4/5) | Moderate (2/5) | Clarinet thumb bears full weight |
| Neck/shoulder load | Low (2/5) | High (4/5) | Horn loads deltoid, pecs, biceps [23] |
| **Overall awkwardness** | **12/25** | **17/25** | Horn is ~1.4× more awkward |

This scale is constructed from the research data, not directly measured in any single study. Individual factors are weighted equally, which may not reflect actual fatigue contribution.

---

## Caveats

- jennyclarinet.com was inaccessible during research [11]. The "70% of new students report hand/wrist discomfort" statistic could not be verified and is not cited in the rubric.
- ErgoBrass data [9] is testimonial, not epidemiological.
- Horn Matters [10] provides qualitative advice without quantified measurements.
- The awkwardness scale above is constructed, not validated by any peer-reviewed study.
- Embouchure forces from Clemente et al. 2019 (within [12]) measure lip force against mouthpiece/reed — they do not capture the full picture of facial muscle fatigue.
